title: Michigan's explosive outbreak of diarrheal parasite jumps to over 1,200 cases
gist: Michigan is experiencing an unprecedented Cyclospora outbreak exceeding 1,251 cases (25x its typical annual total) with cases spreading into Ohio, source still unidentified, and produce the suspected vehicle.

themes.theme: Scale and trajectory of the outbreak

insights.claim: Michigan reported 1,251 cyclosporiasis cases as of July 9 with 44 hospitalized, versus typical yearly totals around 50, making July alone already 25 times the usual annual number.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: A 25x anomaly points to a concentrated contamination source in the food supply, a recall/traceability risk for produce suppliers.
insights.locator: While it is still unclear whether this will be a record year

insights.claim: Cases are spreading across the border into northwestern Ohio, with regional counties reporting more than 500 cases and Lucas County alone at 306.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Multi-state spread from a common source suggests a distributed produce vehicle, widening potential recall scope.
insights.locator: Cases are also surging across the border

insights.claim: The US logs 2,000-5,000 Cyclospora cases annually, with the parasite thriving in summer and cases peaking in June and July.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Seasonal baseline context frames how far Michigan's outbreak exceeds norms.

insights.claim: Cyclospora spreads through contaminated produce and water, not person-to-person, and requires one to two weeks in the environment after fecal shedding to become infectious.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: The non-contagious, food-borne mechanism concentrates risk in the supply chain rather than community spread.

insights.claim: Officials advise avoiding bagged lettuce in favor of whole heads, thoroughly washing herbs and snow peas, and cooking or freezing raspberries, which the parasite clings to and resists cleaning.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: The named high-risk items (bagged greens, herbs, berries) flag which produce categories face demand and reputational risk during outbreaks.
